hook: The world's densest cluster of secondhand bookshops
facts: Around 176 used bookstores cluster here, roughly a third of all Japan's secondhand booksellers
Grew up serving Meiji-era universities and schools that opened nearby from the 1870s
Most shops face north so sunlight never fades the spines on the pavement carts
Publishers Shogakukan, Shueisha and Iwanami Shoten keep their headquarters here
w: Built around universities that opened after 1868, rebuilt after fire and the 1923 quake, this warren now holds the planet's thickest concentration of used books. Shops hyper-specialise: Noh booklets here, war manga there. Most face north so the sun won't fade the spines on the outdoor carts. Bibliophile gravity well.

hook: A 170-metre Showa shopping street that the bombs missed
facts: Formed spontaneously around 1945 as roughly 60 family-run shops
One of few Tokyo districts to survive both the 1923 quake and the wartime firebombing
Seven hand-carved wooden cats hide on the awnings and rooftops along the street
Residents resisted postwar high-rise redevelopment to keep the street's old scale
legend: Locals say the cats trace to Edo-era temples that kept felines to fight the mice
w: Spared by both the 1923 quake and the wartime fires, this 170-metre run of about 60 family shops kept the low Showa scale that the rest of Tokyo bulldozed. Croquettes fry, old men gossip, and seven hand-carved wooden cats hide on the awnings. They say the strays descend from temple cats kept to hunt mice. Cat town that refused to grow up.

hook: Tokyo's only restaurant cooking indigenous Ainu cuisine
facts: Opened in 2011 by Teruyo Usa, an Ainu woman from Hokkaido
Serves ohaw, a wild-vegetable and salmon soup once an Ainu staple
Ingredients including kitopiro herbs and shikerebe berries come from Hokkaido
Doubles as a gathering place for Tokyo's Ainu community
w: Under the thundering Shin-Okubo rail line, behind a door like someone's home, sits the only place in Tokyo cooking the food of Japan's indigenous Ainu. Teruyo Usa opened it in 2011: ohaw soup, venison, kitopiro dumplings, bitter shikerebe berries from Hokkaido. Part kitchen, part hearth for a culture the state spent a century erasing.

hook: A postwar black-market warren named for its harmonica-narrow lanes
facts: Grew from a flea market that sprang up by the station in 1945
Named because the rows of tiny stalls resembled a harmonica's reeds
Around 90 shops and bars now pack the cramped passageways
Some consider it the first black market to appear after the war
w: It started in 1945 as a black market in the rubble outside Kichijoji station, and the lanes grew so tight and the stalls so regular that people likened them to a harmonica's reeds. Some 90 bars and shops now jam the gaps, smoke and yakitori everywhere. The bubble nearly killed it; the drinkers brought it back.

hook: Tokyo's stubbornly unchanged capital of punk and vintage
facts: First-wave Japanese punk bands gathered here from the late 1970s for cheap rent
Over 100 vintage clothing shops cluster south of the station
Live-house lore runs from 20000V to the long-running basement Penguin House
Filled with small merchants displaced by the 1923 Great Kanto Earthquake
w: While Shibuya razed itself every twenty years, Koenji barely budged: same buildings, often the same owners, the same awkward crowd. Punk bands settled here from the late 1970s for cheap rent and sympathetic live-house owners, and the racks of a hundred-odd vintage shops still spill onto the lanes. Tokyo's most bloody-minded neighbourhood.

hook: A hidden tea-garden of teahouses behind a Kuma-designed gallery
facts: The 17,000-square-metre garden holds four tearooms among rolling wooded hills
Kengo Kuma rebuilt the gallery in 2009, wrapping it in coral-grey stone and bamboo
Holds a collection of around 7,600 East Asian artworks, some over 3,000 years old
Sits steps from the fashion towers of Omotesando
w: Step past Kengo Kuma's bamboo-lined gallery and the city stops: 17,000 square metres of mossy slopes, stone lanterns and four secretive teahouses, a stone's throw from Omotesando's glass towers. Built around a railway baron's hoard of East Asian art. Maples in autumn, stillness all year. An oasis that charges admission and earns it.

hook: A 1916 machiya kissaten revived by its own devotees
facts: Housed in a 1916 timber machiya that survived the 1923 quake and wartime air raids
Ran as Kayaba Coffee from 1938 until it closed in 2006
Reopened in 2009 by regulars who refused to let it disappear
Famous for its tamago sando and a coffee-and-cocoa Russian Coffee
w: A timber corner shop from 1916, dodging earthquake and firebomb alike, pouring coffee under the name Kayaba since 1938. When it shut in 2006 the regulars couldn't bear it and reopened the place themselves in 2009. Egg sandwiches, cream sodas, and a coffee-and-cocoa concoction they call Russian. The kissaten as community heirloom.

hook: A 200-year-old public bath turned contemporary gallery
facts: The building served as the Kashiwayu bathhouse, established around 1787
Converted to a contemporary art gallery in 1993
Wooden geta lockers and the bather-screening curved entrance survive inside
Has shown Anish Kapoor, Louise Bourgeois, Kohei Nawa and Tadanori Yokoo
w: Behind a tiled roof and tall chimney, the Kashiwayu bathhouse washed Yanaka's neighbours from around 1787. Since 1993 it has shown Kapoor, Bourgeois and Yokoo instead, white walls rising where the tubs once steamed. The wooden geta lockers stay; so does the curved entry built to keep bathers from prying eyes. Soaking replaced by staring.

hook: A fifth-floor shrine to 1980s and 90s gaming
facts: Hidden on the fifth floor of an unassuming Shinjuku-Sanchome building
Owner Nao Fukuda has amassed consoles from Famicom to Mega Drive and Virtual Boy
The shelves include the notorious Power Glove and a Game Boy display
Chiptune soundtracks play while guests pick games to play with their drinks
w: Five floors up an anonymous Shinjuku-Sanchome stairwell, owner Nao Fukuda hoards the gaming junk of two decades: Famicom, Mega Drive, the doomed Virtual Boy, even the useless Power Glove. Chiptune blips over the bar while you pull a cartridge off the shelf and drink a cocktail named for some pixel hero. A childhood, decanted.

hook: A tiki-grotto kissaten in book town since 1955
facts: Pouring coffee in Jimbocho since 1955
Decor mixes cabin timber, tiki carvings and gifts left by decades of regulars
A semi-underground room gives the cottage-fronted cafe a grotto feel
Known for pizza toast and a multicoloured cream soda
w: A log-cabin front in book town opens onto a semi-underground grotto crammed with tiki carvings, red telephones and trinkets regulars have left since 1955. The blend comes dark, the cream soda in unnatural colours. Western kitsch, Polynesian fantasy and pure Showa kissaten, fused into one delirious basement. Coffee as time capsule.

hook: A backstreet shrine with a climbable miniature Mount Fuji
facts: Holds a fujizuka, a lava-stone Mount Fuji built in 1828, around six metres high
The mound counts as an Important Tangible Folk Cultural Property
Built with real lava hauled from Mount Fuji itself
Opened for climbing only on a couple of days around late June and July
legend: Climbing the mound was held to earn the same blessing as scaling the real Fuji
w: On a quiet Shitaya backstreet sits a six-metre Mount Fuji, heaped in 1828 from real lava dragged off the volcano so commoners barred from the climb could earn the blessing near home. Devotees believed a lap of this cone equalled the true ascent. Fenced off most of the year, it opens two summer days. Pocket pilgrimage.

hook: Hundreds of world drums, most of which you may strike
facts: Opened in 1988 above the Miyamoto Unosuke drum-maker's shop
Holds hundreds of percussion instruments from around the world
Blue-dot drums may be played; red-dot ones are too fragile to touch
Run by a family firm of taiko makers founded in 1861
w: Above an Asakusa shop making taiko since 1861, one floor crams hundreds of drums from every continent, and the rule turns the visit upside down: anything with a blue dot, you may hit. Red dots stay off-limits, too rare to bruise. Children flail, parents flinch, a Ghanaian talking drum mutters back. A museum that wants noise.
